english: sometimes gáṅ-po (gang po), also gaṅ (gang) 1. full, rín-po-čés (rin po ches) baṅ-mdzód (bang mdzod) gáṅ-ba (gang ba) žig (zhig) a treasury full of jewels Dzl.; tál-ču (thal chu) kól-mas (khol mas) gáṅ-ba-ste (gang ba ste) being filled with boiling lye Thgy.; gser-pyé (gser phye) bre (bre) gáṅ-po (gang po), gser (gser) gžóṅ-pa (gzhong pa) gaṅ (gang) a measure filled with gold-dust, a basin full of gold; óbs-kyi ('obs kyi) náṅ-na (nang na) sbrul (sbrul) gdúg-pas (gdug pas) gáṅ-ṅo (gang ngo) lit.: in the ditch it was full of poisonous snakes Dzl.; brgyud (brgyud) gáṅ-bar (gang bar) gyúr-to (gyur to) the progeny increased Glr.; mčód-rten (mchod rten) ḳru (khru) gáṅ-pa (gang pa) Glr. a pyramid, a full cubit in height. - 2. W. also heaped (measure), opp. to "gaṅ-čád" (gang chad) (lit. bčad (bcad)) smoothed (measure).

pod, shell, husk (Sch. also flower-bud?) _od-zér-gyi ('od zer gyi) gáṅ-bur (gang bur) dril-nas (dril nas) enveloping himself in a veil of rays, wrapping himself in a garment of light (another reading: góṅ-por (gong por) in a lump, in one mass) Glr.; gaṅ-ló (gang lo) an empty pod, freed from the kernels W.

1. man, as an intellectual being, a person; gaṅ-zág (gang zag) gžán-gyis (gzhan gyis) brda (brda) sprád-pas (sprad pas) another person describing it to you (opp. to what we know by our own perception and observation) Mil.; hence philosophical term for the I or self, Was.; bstan-bčós-la (bstan bcos la) mkás-pai (mkhas pai) gaṅ-zág-rnams (gang zag rnams) learned or lettered men, men of science Glr.; esp. man in relation to religion: čos (chos) pyi-bšól (phyi bshol) byéd-pai (byed pai) gaṅ-zág (gang zag) Mil., men who postpone religion, not troubling themselves about it: págs-pai ('phags pai) gaṅ-zág-rnams-kyi (gang zag rnams kyi) rgyál-po (rgyal po) the king of reverend persons, i.e. Buddha; lóg-lta-čan-gyi (log lta can gyi) gaṅ-zág (gang zag) heretical people; gaṅ-zág (gang zag) pál-pa (phal pa), ṭa-mál-pá (tha mal pa) common people Mil. and others; also explicitly: people favourably disposed towards religion, religious people Gyatch. c. 26 & 27. (at present the word is generally understood in the latter sense); dus (dus) pyís-kyi (phyis kyi) gaṅ-zág (gang zag) Glr., ma-_óṅs-pai (ma 'ongs pai) gaṅ-zág (gang zag) skál-ba (skal ba) daṅ (dang) ldán-pa (ldan pa) Mil. a pious posterity. The word, however, so little implies the clerical state, that it is used directly for 2. layman, one that has not taken orders Dzl. 2S°, 5 and elsewhere. - 3. (resp. žal-zág (zhal zag)) tobacco-pipe, not the hukka, but a small sort, similar to ours, gen. made of metal; gaṅ-mgó (gang mgo) bowl of a tobacco-pipe; gaṅ-mjúg (gang mjug) mouth-piece or tip of it C.

1. glacier-ice, glacier; gáṅs-čan (gangs can) adj. abounding in snow, in glaciers, also as a sbst. a glacier; gáṅs-čan-las (gangs can las) _byúṅ-bai ('byung bai) ču (chu) the water issuing from a glacier Med., and even as a p.n.: Tibet; gáṅs-čan-gyi (gangs can gyi) skad (skad) the Tibetan language; gáṅs-bsóg-pa (gangs bsog pa) to cleave the snow, i.e. to have it trodden down by yaks sent in advance, in order thus to form a path for the travellers (v. Huc Voyage II. 421). - gaṅs-rgyúd (gangs rgyud) a chain of snow-mountains. - gaṅs-čén-mzod-lṅá (gangs chen mzod lnga) 'the five receptacles of the vast glacier-ice', or gaṅs-čén-rjé-lṅá (gangs chen rje lnga) 'the five kings of the same', pronounced *ghaṅ-čen-_dzo'-ṅá* (ghang chen 'dzo' nga), or *je-ṅá* (je nga), n. of a high mountain in Sikkim, commonly spelled Kinjinjunga; gaṅs-čén-mtsó-rgyál (gangs chen mtsho rgyal) name of a deity (?) Glr. - gaṅs-tígs (gangs thigs) Med. perh. stalactite. - gaṅs-rí (gangs ri) a snow- or ice-mountain, as p.n. = Ti-se (Ti se). - Seldom 2. col. ice in general; *gaṅs-soṅ* (gangs song) it has frozen W. - 3. snow in general, *ghaṅ _bab* (ghang 'bab) it snows Ts.; *ghaṅ-ma-čár* (ghang ma char) sleet. - 4. the sclerotic of the eye Sch.

1. a bluff; precipitous river-banks, such as frequently inclose the mountain rivers of Tibet. - 2. In W. the word seems to refer more to the species of rock, which is favourable to the formation of such banks: conglomerate; gad-púg (gad phug) a cavern in such a bank; gad-rgyál (gad rgyal) the gigantic walls of conglomerate rock, through which mountain rivers have cut their way.

laughing, laughter, _jig-rtén-pai ('jig rten pai) gád-mo (gad mo) a laughter, a laughing-stock, to wordly-minded people; ṅai (ngai) gád-moi (gad moi) gnas (gnas) this is to me an object of laughing, it is ridiculous to me Mil.; gád-mos (gad mos) _débs-pa ('debs pa) to laugh at a person Tar. 25, 15.

B. and W., gám (gam) C., nearness, proximity, used only in such connections as gan-du (gan du) to, towards, up to, ṅai (ngai) gán-du (gan du) šog (shog) come to me; rgyál-poi (rgyal poi) gán-du (gan du) he went to the king; kăṅ-pai (khang pai) gán-du (gan du) soṅ (song) he went
